summary: Jeff Atwood introduces readers to Bayesian reasoning through a classic medical probability puzzle that stumps 85% of doctors. He explains that while Bayes' theorem is counterintuitive and difficult for humans to grasp naturally, it has immense practical power—most notably as the foundation for modern spam filtering. Atwood goes further to introduce Markovian filtering as an even more effective technique that examines word phrases rather than individual words. The post ultimately argues that Bayesian reasoning extends far beyond spam detection and can be applied to discriminate between almost anything, explaining the fervent enthusiasm the theorem generates among its adherents.
key_points: Only 15% of doctors correctly solve a basic Bayesian probability problem, demonstrating how counterintuitive this reasoning is for humans
Bayes' theorem is the foundation for virtually all modern spam filters, first popularized by Paul Graham in 2002
Markovian filtering improves on Bayesian methods by examining relationships between phrases rather than individual words, using exponential weighting for longer spans
These statistical discrimination techniques can be applied far beyond spam to classify web pages, resumes, blog entries, log files, and more with up to 99.9% accuracy
Bayesian reasoning has generated a devoted following who view it as foundational to understanding probability and even the scientific method itself
money_quotes: Only 15% of doctors, when presented with this situation, come up with the correct answer.
Bayesian reasoning is apparently one of those things which, like quantum mechanics or the Wason Selection Test, is inherently difficult for humans to grasp with our built-in mental faculties.
In computer science, it's easy to demonstrate the immense power of Bayes' theorem: it's the basis for almost all spam filters in use today.
CRM114 learns, and it learns fast.
What is the so-called Bayesian Revolution now sweeping through the sciences, which claims to subsume even the experimental method itself as a special case?
What matters is that Bayes is cool, and if you don't know Bayes, you aren't cool.
key_insight: Bayes' theorem is counterintuitive for humans yet extraordinarily powerful in practice, forming the foundation of spam filtering and countless other classification problems that computers can now solve with remarkable accuracy.
